word_list.pronunciation: æz ɔːɹ sˈoʊ fˌɑːɹ æz ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɪz kənsˈɜːnd

senses.definition: used to refer to the specific matter or topic being discussed or considered
used to express one's uncertainty about the statement one has made as there might be something that makes it untrue

examples.x: As far as we know, the company is expanding its operations.
As far as he knew, the concert had already started when he arrived.
They are planning a surprise party for her, as far as he knows.
The company is expanding its operations, as far as we know.
Far as he knows, they are planning a surprise party for her.
Far as I know, she works as a teacher.

senses.definition: used to introduce a statement that presents a truth or reality, often to clarify or emphasize something

examples.x: As a matter of fact, I did see him at the store earlier today.
He claimed it was the largest in the city, but as a matter of fact, there are several larger buildings in the downtown area.
You mentioned she wasn't interested; as a matter of fact, she expressed keen interest in joining the project
You may believe it's a rumor, but as a matter of fact, the company has officially announced the merger

word_list.pronunciation: æz ɐ ɔːɹ ɪn pˈɔɪnt ʌv fˈækt

senses.definition: used to introduce a statement that provides factual information or clarifies a point with accuracy

examples.x: The media often portrays the situation inaccurately; in point of fact, crime rates have been steadily declining over the past decade.
As a point of fact, the company's revenue increased by 20% last quarter, contrary to rumors of decline.
In point of fact, the company was founded in 1995, not 1997 as commonly believed.
As a point of fact, the research study was conducted over a period of three years, not two.
In point of fact, the Earth is not perfectly round; it's slightly flattened at the poles and bulges at the equator.
As a point of fact, the theory has been widely accepted by the scientific community due to substantial evidence supporting it.

senses.definition: used to introduce a statement that provides additional information or emphasizes the truth or reality of a situation

examples.x: She said she would be late; in fact, she didn't arrive until well after the meeting had started.
The project seemed simple, but in fact, it required extensive research and planning.
She was confident about the facts, and in fact, her research was comprehensive and accurate.
We thought the process would take a week, but in fact, it was completed in just a few days.
He told me he didn't know her; in fact, they are close friends.
She is fluent in Spanish and French and, in addition, speaks a little Italian.
The project was praised for its innovative design and, in addition, its cost-effectiveness.
The event was well-organized; the decorations, in addition, were stunning.

senses.definition: used to contrast with appearances or assumptions, emphasizing the actual state of affairs or the way things truly are

examples.x: In reality, the situation is more complex than it appears on the surface.
She seems confident, but in reality, she's struggling with self-doubt.
The project appeared simple at first glance, in reality, it was much more complex.
In reality, their relationship is strained despite their outward displays of affection.
The politician promised change, but in reality, little progress has been made.

senses.definition: used to emphasize that something is being expressed honestly or genuinely, often revealing the real facts or feelings

examples.x: In truth, I have always admired your dedication and hard work.
She seemed confident, and in truth, she was well-prepared for the presentation.
He claimed to be an expert, but in truth, his knowledge on the subject was limited.
The situation appeared complicated, but in truth, it was a straightforward misunderstanding.

senses.definition: used to introduce an explanation or clarification of a point just made

examples.x: The project must be completed by the end of the month, that is to say, within the next two weeks.
He enjoys outdoor activities, such as hiking and camping—that is to say, he prefers to spend time in nature.
The event will take place next Friday, that is to say, on the 20th of May.
The software update will enhance security features, that is to say, it will improve protection against cyber threats.
The company aims to expand its market share by targeting younger demographics—that is to say, individuals aged between 18 and 35.

senses.definition: used to indicate something that is typically or generally true or customary

examples.x: As a rule, we start our meetings at 9:00 AM sharp.
As a rule, I avoid eating sugary snacks to maintain a healthy diet.
As a rule, the company offers flexible working hours to its employees.
As a rule, he prefers to arrive early for appointments to avoid being late.
As a rule, they take their dog for a walk every morning before breakfast.

senses.definition: used to connect two related statements, showing that the same reasoning or principle applies to both

examples.x: He enjoys spending time outdoors, and by the same token, he dislikes being cooped up indoors for too long.
She believes in treating others with kindness and respect, and by the same token, she expects to be treated the same way.
If you expect your employees to work hard, then by the same token, you should provide them with adequate support and resources.
If you expect your children to follow the rules, then by the same token, you should set a good example for them to follow.
